---
title: "The Effect of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Risk of Corruption"
subtitle: "Evidence from Mexico's Federal Procurement System"
author: "Adan Silverio-Murillo, Daniel Prudencio & Jose Roberto Balmori-de-la-Miyar"
institute: "Published in <em>Public Organization Review</em>, 2024"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    embed-resources: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
---

## The Puzzle: Did COVID-19 Increase Corruption Risk?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="57%"}
**The concern:** Emergency spending + relaxed oversight = [corruption opportunity]{.negative}

- Governments worldwide rapidly increased procurement budgets
- Accountability standards were explicitly relaxed
- High-profile cases of overpriced medical equipment surfaced quickly

**The natural expectation:** [Healthcare institutions]{.negative} would be at the center of pandemic corruption, most money, most relaxed rules, most pressure

**The research question:** Did COVID-19 measurably increase corruption risk in Mexico's federal procurement, and if so, [where?]{.hi-gold}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.methodbox}
**The setting:**

Mexico's public contracts can be allocated through:

1. [Open public auction]{.hi} (law's preferred method)
2. [Invitation auction]{.hi-gold} (selected suppliers only)
3. [Direct allocation]{.hi-gold} (no competition)

Mechanisms 2 and 3 are legally allowed but discretionary, opening the door to favoritism
:::

::: {.keybox}
**Outcome:** DCVB ratio, the share of total contract value assigned without competitive bidding
:::
:::
:::

## The Data: Mexico's Federal Procurement System

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Data source:** CompraNet, Mexico's public procurement system, systematized by IMCO

**Sample:** Balanced panel of institutions, January 2018 to December 2020

| Feature | Value |
|:--------|------:|
| Federal institutions | 64 |
| Public acquisitions | 378,000 |
| Institution-month obs. | 2,304 |
| Pre-COVID DCVB (Overall) | 0.705 |
| Pre-COVID DCVB (Health) | 0.667 |
| Pre-COVID DCVB (Non-Health) | 0.715 |

*Source: IMCO*

::: {.smallest}
The 64 institutions cover approximately 75% of all federal public acquisition allocations
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.keybox}
**Key baseline fact:**

Before the pandemic, [70% of contract value]{.hi-gold} was already assigned through discretionary mechanisms. This is the status quo the pandemic disrupted.
:::

::: {.methodbox}
**Two groups of institutions:**

[Healthcare:]{.hi-gold} federal hospitals, IMSS, health secretariats. Intense public scrutiny during the pandemic.

[Non-healthcare:]{.hi-gold} defense, education, infrastructure, energy, and other sectors. Comparatively less media attention.
:::
:::
:::

## How Do We Identify the Effect of the Pandemic?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="53%"}
**Difference-in-Differences:**

The pandemic is a universal shock, all institutions were affected. Variation comes from the time dimension:

- [Treatment indicator:]{.hi-gold} 1 if year = 2020, 0 for 2018–2019
- [Time indicator:]{.hi-gold} 1 for March–December, 0 for January–February
- [COVID-19 variable:]{.hi-gold} interaction = 1 for March–December 2020

$$\text{DCVB}_{imy} = \alpha + \beta^{DD}\,\text{COVID19}_{imy} + a_i + \gamma_m + \nu_y + e_{imy}$$

$\beta^{DD}$ captures the average effect of the pandemic months on the DCVB ratio

**Event study:** Traces the dynamic effect month by month, and validates the parallel trends assumption
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}

<svg viewBox="0 0 460 270" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-height:240px;">
  <!-- Grid -->
  <line x1="80" y1="40" x2="80" y2="230" stroke="#ccc" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="270" y1="40" x2="270" y2="230" stroke="#012169" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/>
  <line x1="80" y1="230" x2="440" y2="230" stroke="#ccc" stroke-width="1"/>
  <!-- X axis labels -->
  <text x="175" y="248" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">Jan–Feb 2020</text>
  <text x="355" y="248" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">Mar–Dec 2020</text>
  <!-- Lockdown label -->
  <text x="270" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#012169" font-family="sans-serif">Lockdown</text>
  <!-- Pre period label -->
  <text x="175" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif">Pre-period</text>
  <!-- Post period label -->
  <text x="355" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#012169" font-family="sans-serif" font-weight="bold">Post-period</text>
  <!-- Counterfactual line (dashed) -->
  <line x1="90" y1="160" x2="430" y2="160" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="435" y="163" font-size="9" fill="#999" font-family="sans-serif">CF</text>
  <!-- Observed line -->
  <line x1="90" y1="160" x2="270" y2="160" stroke="#012169" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="270" y1="160" x2="355" y2="115" stroke="#B91C1C" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <line x1="355" y1="115" x2="430" y2="145" stroke="#B91C1C" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- Arrow showing beta -->
  <line x1="355" y1="118" x2="355" y2="157" stroke="#B91C1C" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="5" markerHeight="5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 Z" fill="#B91C1C"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="365" y="142" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#B91C1C" font-family="sans-serif">β</text>
  <!-- Peak label -->
  <text x="355" y="108" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#B91C1C" font-family="sans-serif">Peak (q=2)</text>
  <!-- Y axis label -->
  <text x="30" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#555" font-family="sans-serif" transform="rotate(-90,30,150)">DCVB ratio</text>
  <!-- Y axis ticks -->
  <line x1="76" y1="160" x2="84" y2="160" stroke="#999" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="72" y="163" text-anchor="end" font-size="9" fill="#999" font-family="sans-serif">0.705</text>
</svg>

::: {.smallest}
Institution ($a_i$), month ($\gamma_m$), and year ($\nu_y$) fixed effects absorb all time-invariant differences. Standard errors clustered at the institution level.
:::
:::
:::

## Main Result: It Was Not Healthcare

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="54%"}
**Difference-in-Differences results (Table 2):**

|  | Overall | Health | Non-Health |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1(COVID-19) | 0.120\*\*\* | −0.060 | 0.166\*\*\* |
|  | (0.034) | (0.043) | (0.039) |
| N | 2,304 | 468 | 1,836 |
| R² | 0.32 | 0.19 | 0.35 |
| Pre-COVID mean | 0.705 | 0.667 | 0.715 |
| % change | +17.02% | −9.09% | +23.21% |

*\*\*\* p<0.01. Source: IMCO.*

::: {.smallest}
Baseline fixed effects at the institution, month, and year level
:::
:::

::: {.column width="43%"}
::: {.resultbox}
**The counterintuitive result:**

The pandemic increased discretionary contracting by [+17%]{.hi-gold} overall.

This is [entirely driven by non-healthcare institutions (+23%)]{.hi-gold}.

Healthcare institutions show [no significant change]{.negative}, despite being at the center of the public debate.
:::

::: {.keybox}
A possible explanation: intense media and civil society scrutiny of healthcare procurement may have [constrained discretionary behavior]{.hi} precisely where it was most expected.
:::
:::
:::

## Dynamic Effects and Mechanisms

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Event study (Figure 1):** Inverted U-shape for non-health

- Pre-pandemic coefficients are [not statistically significant]{.hi-gold}, supporting parallel trends
- Effect [peaks at q=2]{.hi-gold} (May 2020, when stay-at-home order was lifted)
- Non-healthcare DCVB rises by [20%]{.hi-gold} by the second month
- Effect persists for [four subsequent months]{.hi-gold}, then returns to pre-pandemic levels
- Healthcare DCVB: [flat throughout]{.negative}

Consistent with the [resilience of crime theory]{.hi}: a temporary window of reduced oversight, not a permanent shift
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Two mechanisms investigated:**

::: {.methodbox}
**Market concentration (HHI)**

A Herfindahl-Hirschman Index is constructed for each institution-month. Non-healthcare institutions saw their supplier market become significantly more concentrated during the pandemic.
:::

::: {.methodbox}
**Late disclosure of contract information**

The proportion of contracts with procedural information published after the contract start date increased for non-healthcare institutions, consistent with reduced transparency.
:::

::: {.smallest}
The leave-one-out analysis confirms results are not driven by any single institution, including SEDENA (the Mexican Army)
:::
:::
:::

## Conclusions and Policy Implications

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we found:**

1. COVID-19 increased discretionary contracting in Mexico's federal procurement by [+17%]{.hi-gold} overall
2. The effect is driven entirely by [non-healthcare institutions (+23%)]{.hi-gold}, not by healthcare
3. The increase was [temporary]{.hi}: peaked two months after the stay-at-home order lifted, returned to baseline within six months
4. Non-healthcare institutions also became [more concentrated]{.hi-gold} in their supplier base and [less transparent]{.hi-gold} in contract disclosure
:::

::: {.column width="47%"}
::: {.keybox}
**Policy implication:**

Audit efforts targeting pandemic-era procurement should focus on [non-healthcare institutions]{.hi-gold}, even though the political debate centered on healthcare.

Public scrutiny may itself be a corruption deterrent: the sectors that receive [less attention]{.negative} are where the risk actually materializes.
:::

::: {.smallest}
**Limitation:** DCVB measures [corruption risk]{.hi-gold}, not actual corruption. A rising share of discretionary contracts is consistent with both legitimate emergency procurement and corrupt favoritism. The paper identifies a pattern at the aggregate level, not individual wrongdoing.
:::
:::
:::
